pathophysiology_summary: Bullous pemphigoid is a subepidermal autoimmune blistering disease (anti-BP180/BP230 hemidesmosome antibodies) of older adults, producing tense, clear, serous bullae — classically on the lower legs and flexures — on a mildly urticarial/erythematous base, with a negative Nikolsky sign and no mucosal involvement (contrast with flaccid, Nikolsky-positive pemphigus vulgaris). Localised disease responds to potent topical corticosteroids. In an aged-care resident, acute tense bullae with surrounding erythema are readily mistaken for bullous cellulitis; the decisive discriminator is the absence of systemic toxicity, spreading warmth, induration and pain. The main hazard is secondary infection of de-roofed blisters in frail skin.

differential.evidence_basis: Tense clear bullae on both lower legs with mild surrounding erythema in an elderly patient, minimal pain/itch.
Infection must be excluded in a frail host — screen for fever, spreading warmth, pain and systemic illness.
Distinguished by flaccid bullae, positive Nikolsky sign and mucosal involvement — absent here.
Less likely without chronic venous change/haemosiderin/weeping eczema.
Afebrile, no spreading warmth/pain (DI-001); tense clear bullae, no mucosal involvement, no new drug (DI-002) — classic localised bullous pemphigoid.
No fever, spreading warmth, induration or systemic toxicity — acute cellulitis not supported.
Tense (not flaccid) bullae and no mucosal disease.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: potent topical corticosteroid, protect intact blister roofs, nurse skin monitoring, prompt in-person GP/dermatology review (biopsy/immunofluorescence), and a cellulitis safety-net — NOT empirical antibiotics or ED transfer in the absence of infection signs.

red_flags.description: Cellulitis/systemic infection — fever, spreading warmth/erythema, increasing pain, cloudy/purulent blister fluid, or systemic decline
red_flags.clinical_significance: Secondary infection in frail skin can progress to sepsis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated cellulitis/sepsis in a frail elderly resident causes rapid deterioration.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Assuming erythematous blistering legs in an elderly resident must be cellulitis and prescribing antibiotics/ED transfer without checking for systemic infection signs.
Failing to arrange nurse skin checks and a cellulitis safety-net, risking a missed secondary infection.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of warmth, induration and tenderness
Bulla aspiration and skin biopsy / direct immunofluorescence
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Topical steroid + protect blister roofs + facility nurse monitoring + prompt in-person GP/dermatology review; ED only if infection signs develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Mislabelling as cellulitis leads to unnecessary antibiotics/transfer; missing a genuine secondary infection in frail skin risks sepsis.
